title: Superforecasting: The Art and Science of Prediction
author: Philip E. Tetlock, Dan Gardner
excerpt: A decade-long study revealed that some people can predict future events with remarkable accuracy—not because they have special knowledge, but because they think in specific, learnable ways. Tetlock shows how the discipline of probabilistic forecasting can sharpen judgment in business, politics, and everyday life.
keyIdeas: Superforecasters are made, not born—their skills can be learned
Good forecasting requires probabilistic thinking, not binary predictions
Humility, intellectual curiosity, and willingness to update beliefs are key
The Good Judgment Project proved that training improves forecasting accuracy
keyTakeaways: Update your beliefs incrementally as new evidence arrives
Break problems down into their component parts
Seek out perspectives that contradict your own
whoShouldRead: Anyone who makes predictions or judgments under uncertainty
People interested in improving their critical thinking
Fans of Thinking, Fast and Slow who want the practical application
whoShouldSkip: Readers who want simple, black-and-white answers
